retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal seizures are classified into several electroclinical types, each with distinct physical features. Accurate recognition of seizure semiology is essential for diagnosis, especially when electroencephalographic (EEG) correlation is limited. The major seizure types and their characteristic features are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: EEG Neonatal Visual Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: A neonatal epilepsy disorder can point towards a more ominous sign of sustained and permanent brain damage. Early detection, management, and aggressive treatment of seizures and status epilepticus can prevent long term neurological sequela. About 3/1000 neonates will have a seizure disorder in the first two days of life in term babies and up to 132/1000 in pre-term infants. Neonatal seizures can be classified as subtle, tonic, clonic, generalized tonic-clonic, myoclonic, with behavioral features that can be paroxysmal, non-paroxysmal, repetitive, and stereotypical.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal seizures constitute a commonly encountered neurologic condition in neonates. [1] Defined as sudden, paroxysmal, and abnormal alterations of electrographic activity from birth to the end of the neonatal period, these events reflect the developmental immaturity of the neonatal brain. This immaturity contributes to a unique pathophysiology and electrographic pattern, resulting in clinical manifestations that can be different and more difficult to identify than in older age groups. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Seizure activity is more common during the newborn period than at any other stage of life. However, normal newborn behaviors can sometimes mimic seizures, complicating diagnosis. Premature neonates experience seizures more frequently, often presenting with less organized patterns. A multicenter study by Murray et al found that many seizure-like events were either unrecognized or misinterpreted. [34] Clinical evaluation at the bedside alone is often insufficient and typically requires confirmatory EEG monitoring. [34] Inaccurate diagnosis of neonatal seizures can have serious consequences, as both undertreatment and overtreatment may adversely affect the neurological development of neonates.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In some cases, these abnormal motor phenomena are the first indication of a significant neurologic disorder. [3] Rapid recognition and evaluation are essential to determine the cause, prevent further cerebral injury, and terminate ongoing ictal episodes. [4] [5] This activity provides an overview of the etiologies, clinical features, evaluation, and approach to managing neonatal seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: EEG Neonatal Visual Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Subtle: (approximately 50%) Difficult to detect clinically. It can consist of aleatory roving eye movements, including open fluttering and rolling up. Oral smacking or automatisms, pedal movements, staring/apneic events, repetitive sucking, chewing sounds, and tongue protrusion. Clonic: (approximately 25%)  Involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that are fast frequency. Myoclonic: (approximately 20%) Repetitive brief shock-like jerks of the limbs. Tonic: (approximately 5%) Repetitive and sustained tonic/stiffening flexion or extension of the limbs, with decerebrate and decorticate like posturing. Generalized tonic-clonic: Repetitive alternating tonic-clonic movements that represent a primary or secondarily generalized seizure associated with poor prognosis, but very rare.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Accurate differentiation between epileptic and nonepileptic movements is essential to avoid unnecessary treatment and identify neonates requiring further evaluation. EEG confirmation and thorough clinical assessment are crucial for establishing an accurate diagnosis and guiding effective management.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Seizures are common in neonates, particularly in the first month of life. Neonatal seizures can be either provoked or unprovoked. Provoked seizures, also known as acute symptomatic seizures, may result from hypoxic-ischemic injury, cerebral infarction, brain bleeds, metabolic derangements, or infections. Unprovoked seizures are often due to underlying structural brain abnormalities or genetic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Isolated apnea is more common in premature neonates during sleep and is typically associated with desaturations and bradycardia, usually being nonepileptic in nature. An epileptic seizure should be suspected in term neonates when apnea is accompanied by abnormal eye movements, mouth movements, hypertension, or tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal epilepsy syndromes are a group of electroclinical syndromes that are considered a medical urgency as they may indicate underlying structural or neuro-metabolic derangements. Although some of these disorders improve over time, others can lead to severe neurodevelopmental consequences, resulting in significant morbidity and mortality. [1] [2] Most neonatal seizures that occur in the first month of life are acute symptomatic. Neonatal epilepsy syndromes account for a smaller portion of neonatal seizures. [3] Timely and accurate diagnosis is crucial for initiating appropriate therapy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal EEG -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal electroencephalography (EEG) is a diagnostic procedure utilized to assess neurologic conditions (eg, encephalopathy and seizure disorders) and evaluate neonatal brain maturity. [1] [2] EEG measures the postsynaptic potential of neuronal axons. Neonatologists use this valuable tool to gauge if there are abnormalities present in the normal background brain activity of the neonate. [3] [2] Moreover, neonatal EEG helps pediatric neurology clinicians understand the impact of neurological insults on the infant's brain or identify and confirm the presence of seizures. Neurological evaluation of neonates has many inherent limitations, making it challenging to delineate physiological variations from pathological conditions. General patterns of background activity change with the neonate's gestational age and waking or sleep state; therefore, neonatal EEG interpretation is dependent on these clinical considerations. [4] [5] Complications of the procedure are uncommon but include skin maceration and infection. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other symptoms that may mimic seizures include motor automatisms such as repetitive eye opening, eye deviation, tongue movements, or tonic posturing. Patients exhibiting these symptoms should be thoroughly evaluated for underlying nervous system pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Nonepileptic behaviors should be carefully distinguished from neonatal seizures. [56] These movements can closely resemble epileptic events. Thus, EEG is recommended for accurate characterization. [57] Normal neonatal behaviors that may mimic seizures include sucking motions, hiccuping, and benign neonatal sleep myoclonus, which is physiologic myoclonic activity that occurs during sleep. Other nonepileptic movements, often observed in neonates with systemic illness, include jitteriness, apnea, clonus, tremors, and exaggerated startle responses, such as hyperekplexia. [58] [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal seizures occur in 10 to 30 per 1000 live births for preterm and low-birth-weight infants. [15] Neonatal epilepsy syndromes are diagnosed in 15% of neonates with seizures. Of these, 41% are attributed to underlying structural anomalies, 42% to genetic causes, and 9% involve an overlap of both factors. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal seizures are often challenging to recognize clinically, resulting in poor inter-rater reliability. While EEG remains the gold standard for diagnosis, its ability to capture events during short-term monitoring can be limited. This contributes to challenges in accurately estimating the true incidence of neonatal seizures. However, population studies suggest that neonatal seizures occur in 1 to 3 per 1000 live births for full-term infants. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Inborn errors of metabolism Selected enzyme deficiencies Urea cycle defects Peroxisomal disorders Organic acidemias Amino acid disorders Vitamin and cofactor deficiencies Pyroxidine deficiency Molybdenum cofactor deficiency [18] Thromboembolic events Arterial ischemic stroke [19] Sinus venous thrombosis [20] Other conditions to consider include the following: Neonatal epilepsy syndromes Benign neonatal seizures Benign familial neonatal epilepsy Early myoclonic encephalopathy [21] Early infantile epileptic encephalopathy [22] Genetic epilepsy syndromes [23] Congenital brain malformations Schizencephaly Lissencephaly Holoprosencephaly Hydranencephaly [24] Accurate etiologic classification informs both acute treatment and long-term care planning. Clinical, laboratory, and neuroimaging data integration are critical to this process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1440
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all classified as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, a neonatal disorder. This grouping indicates that both forms of transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes fall under the broader category of neonatal disorders, highlighting their importance in newborn assessment, monitoring, and specialized management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base describes a hierarchical taxonomy of perinatal and neonatal disorders that integrates gestational timing, birth weight, fetal growth, and specific clinical conditions across multiple languages and coding systems. A gestational period of 29–30 weeks is classified as خديج (preterm) and is a subtype of liveborn infant; خديج is causally linked to متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome) and to respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), which is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (newborn disorder). اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is a member of the SMQ group اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة and is associated with عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك (unspecified perinatal infection). Transient neonatal diabetes (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 1 and 2), developmental delay, epilepsy, and other neonatal metabolic disorders are also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة. Disorders relating to short gestation and/or low birthweight are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 509
retrieved_docs.content: developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes within the same diagnostic framework as other neonatal disorders. Overall, the knowledge network connects perinatal insults—such as birth trauma, intracerebral hemorrhage, and foetal undernutrition—to a continuum of disorders ranging from metabolic (transient neonatal diabetes) to neurological (developmental delay, epilepsy, cerebral injury) conditions in infants and newborns. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient care in the neonatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are both mapped to the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that they are classified as neonatal disorders. This mapping underscores that transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes represent related early‑life metabolic and neurological conditions, highlighting the need for integrated recognition and management of such disorders in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 700
retrieved_docs.content: establishing a direct etiologic link between traumatic birth events and intracranial bleeding. Specific neonatal metabolic and neurologic disorders such as “DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder)” and “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes” are each mapped to the umbrella term “اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة,” illustrating that these conditions are considered subtypes of newborn disorders. The “اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)” (Standardized MedDRA Query for neonatal disorders) aggregates a collection of related entities, including the broader group “اضطراب لدى الرضع وحديثي الولادة” (infant and newborn disorder). Members of this SMQ include: - “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at maximal prematurity, weight unspecified), - “نزيف مخي وليدي” (intracranial hemorrhage in the newborn), and - “عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك” (infection in the perinatal period, unspecified). Additional conditions linked to the infant and newborn

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that encompasses a wide range of related conditions, complications, and associated diagnostic components. Several specific forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these neonatal and transient presentations are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Clinically, diabetes is linked to multiple serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is also clinically associated with diabetes, and the procedure of amputation of lower extremity is further linked to this cardiac condition, underscoring the systemic impact of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2520
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is recognized as a specific form of newborn disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), representing a narrower subset within the broader category of neonatal disorders. At the same time, it is classified as a particular manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that this cerebral injury falls under the general umbrella of injuries caused by traumatic events during delivery. Understanding that cerebral injury due to birth trauma is both a distinct neonatal disorder and a type of birth trauma helps clinicians identify, assess, and manage these injuries more effectively in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal diabetes conditions and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are recognized as forms or manifestations of diabetes. In addition, the condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is an inverse‑isa relationship to مرض السكري, meaning that gestational or peripartum diabetes represents a particular clinical presentation of the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes), reflecting that patients with diabetes may also exhibit other pancreatic abnormalities unrelated to the primary diabetic process. Moreover, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (a retinal disorder) and اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), underscoring the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse forms of diabetes—including transient neonatal types and pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance—and their broader systemic associations with pancreatic and retinal pathology, which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: intracranial hemorrhage in the newborn (نزيف مخي وليدي), which is also classified within the neonatal disorders SMQ. Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) appears alongside varicose veins of the lower extremity (تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية) in the Diagnosis/Diseases hierarchy. Diabetes is clinically associated with lower‑extremity outcomes such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم), below‑knee amputation, and the broader procedure “amputation of lower extremity,” which is hierarchically divided into below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة) and above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة). Necrosis of an amputation stump of the right lower limb is linked to ulcer morphology (النَخَر), and varicose veins are frequently associated with ulcer formation (قَرحَة), illustrating common vascular complications. Transient neonatal diabetes mellitus (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL) and the syndrome “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes” are mapped both to the neonatal disorder category

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.
